data.title: US judge blocks Texas app store age law meant to protect children
data.description: WASHINGTON, ‚ÄåDec 23 : A federal judge in Texas on Tuesday blocked Texas from enforcing a new state law aimed at protecting children by requiring app stores and developers to verify the age of users, in a win for Apple , Alphabet's Google and other technology companies. U.S. District Judge Robert Pitman in Austin issued a preliminary injunction against the state‚Äôs App Store Accountability Act, finding the ‚Äåmeasure likely violates the U.S. Constitution's speech protections under the ‚ÄåFirst Amendment. The law, which was set to take effect in January, would require parental consent to download apps or make in-app purchases for users aged below 18. Pitman‚Äôs ruling was a win for the Computer & Communications Industry Association, which sued to stop the law. The order was a setback to Texas and its efforts to regulate smartphone use by ‚Äçchildren and teenagers, part of a broader crackdown by some U.S. states to combat potentially harmful effects of social media on young people. Australia this month became the first country to ban social media for children under 16. The Texas attorney general‚Äôs office did not immediately ‚Äãrespond to a request for comment. Pitman ‚Äåacknowledged concerns about children‚Äôs online safety but said ‚Äúhowever compelling the policy concerns, and however widespread the agreement that the issue must be addressed, the court ‚Äãremains bound by the rule of law.‚Äù The Washington, D.C.-based Computer & Communications Industry Association welcomed the ‚ÅÝorder. The order will "preserve the First Amendment rights ‚Äåof app stores, app developers, parents, and younger internet users,‚Äù said Stephanie Joyce, who ‚Äãheads the group‚Äôs litigation center. ‚ÄúIt also protects parents‚Äô inviolate right to use their own judgment in safeguarding their children online using the myriad tools ‚Äçour members provide.‚Äù Google and Apple, which were not plaintiffs, did not immediately respond to requests ‚ÅÝfor comment. The Computer & Communications Industry Association said the law would have placed ‚Äúburdens on app stores, developers, minors, ‚Äãand parents that are ‚Äåcompletely disproportionate to any harm policymakers were attempting to remedy.‚Äù

data.title: Trump's AI hiring campaign draws interest from 25,000 hopefuls
data.description: WASHINGTON, Dec 23 : Roughly 25,000 people have expressed interest in joining the Trump administration's cadre of engineers known as Tech Force, a senior Trump administration official said on Tuesday, as the U.S. government looks to install staff with artificial ‚Äåintelligence expertise in federal roles. The Trump ‚Äåadministration will use that list to recruit software and data engineers, in addition to other tech roles, said Scott Kupor, director of the U.S. Office of Personnel Management, in a post on X. Reuters could ‚Äçnot independently verify the 25,000 figure.¬Ý The interested candidates will compete for 1,000 spots in the first Tech Force cohort. The recruits will spend two years working on technology ‚Äãprojects inside federal agencies, ‚Äåincluding the Departments of Homeland Security, Veterans Affairs, and Justice, among other government offices, Kupor ‚Äãsaid previously.¬Ý The hiring initiative is part of the Trump administration's ‚ÅÝAI agenda. Previous U.S. ‚Äåpresidents have launched similar initiatives to bring tech ‚Äãtalent into government, including former President Joe Biden. President Donald Trump, in the first months of his ‚Äçsecond term, focused on eliminating government jobs, with exceptions ‚ÅÝfor positions his administration said were necessary to maintain "national security." ‚ÄãTech Force is ‚Äåa departure from the downsizing campaign.¬Ý

data.title: US dollar falters as markets focus on Fed easing over growth data
data.description: NEW ‚ÄåYORK/LONDON, Dec 23 : The U.S. dollar fell on Tuesday in a holiday-shortened week, weighed down by expectations of more rate cuts next year, though it trimmed losses against the yen and euro after data showed strong third-quarter growth in the world's largest economy. The report bolstered views that the Federal Reserve will hold off on cutting rates at its January meeting, with the odds currently at 87 per cent, according to LSEG estimates. U.S. rate futures now expect the next Fed easing to occur in June, with two rate cuts of 25 basis points each priced in for 2026. "We could see a lower dollar, at least in the first quarter next year, because the Fed is going to be increasingly forced to admit that the labor market ‚Äåis not in a good place," said Erik Bregar, director, FX & precious metals risk management at Silver Gold Bull ‚Äåin Toronto. "You have these inflation hawks on the committee that are keeping U.S. rates on the long end a little bit higher than they should be," he added. Tuesday's data showed that U.S. gross domestic product rose at a 4.3 per cent annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic Analysis showed. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at a 3.3 per cent pace in the third quarter. Brian Jacobsen, chief economic strategist at Annex Wealth Management in Brookfield, Wisconsin, however, said the strong GDP number was misleading. "Health care spending drove the GDP gains, which isn't necessarily healthy. It is consistent with where employment gains has been, though," Jacobsen ‚Äçwrote in emailed comments after the data was released. "Businesses are eating through their inventories that they stocked up on earlier in the year so we should see a topping-off of inventories going into 2026." Following the report, the dollar cut its losses against the yen to 156.39 yen, still down 0.4 per cent. JAPAN INTERVENTION WATCH The yen earlier gained against the dollar after the strongest indication yet from authorities of Tokyo's readiness to intervene.¬Ý The Japanese unit hovered near recent lows against major peers, with the threat of intervention keeping yen bears at bay ‚Äãfor now. But near-term yen weakness is likely to persist, analysts ‚Äåsay, as the cautious tone from the Bank of Japan last week hinted at a slow pace of rate hikes next year. Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said Japan has a free hand in dealing with excessive moves in the yen. "I would call it an un-hawkish rate hike last week ‚Äãfrom the Bank of Japan. It feels like every time we go into a BOJ meeting, the market gets too excited that we're going to have the start of a ‚ÅÝrate-hiking cycle but the officials always tamp down those expectations," said Silver Gold ‚ÄåBull's Bregar. "So a lot of the price action is really just people getting out of those bets on the yen going up, after they just keep getting ‚Äãdisappointed." The euro also slid, paring gains against the dollar to $1.1786, still up 0.2 per cent on the day. The dollar index, which measures the U.S. currency against six rivals, slipped 0.2 per cent to 98.07, extending losses into a second day. The index is at its lowest level since early October and ‚Äçon course for a 1.4 per cent decline for the month, its biggest since August, and a 9.6 per cent drop for the year, its steepest annual fall since 2017. Strategists at MUFG said ‚ÅÝthe drop for the dollar this year is unlikely to be a one-off with scope for further gains ahead. The dollar also dipped after U.S. consumer confidence worsened in December. The Conference Board said on ‚ÄãTuesday its consumer confidence index fell 3.8 ‚Äåpoints to 89.1 this month. Economists polled by Reuters had forecast the index at 91.0.

data.title: ServiceNow to buy Armis for $7.75 billion as AI-fueled cyber risks surge
data.description: Dec 23 : ServiceNow on Tuesday agreed to buy cybersecurity startup Armis for $7.75 billion, in the enterprise software maker's biggest-ever deal, as it bolsters its services to help counter rising cyberattacks driven by AI adoption. Through the deal, the company aims to integrate Alphabet-backed Armis' security features such as device scanning and threat detection, a ‚Äåcrucial advantage amid sophisticated cyberattacks that have hit ‚Äåcompanies ranging from Microsoft to UnitedHealth Group. However, ServiceNow's shares fell about 3 per cent, as investors worried over the company's recent splurge on acquisitions. "Our security stack, with the acquisition of Armis, is very well positioned, so we won't need to do any more M&A in security space," ServiceNow Chief Financial Officer Gina Mastantuono ‚Äçtold Reuters. The company expects the deal, scheduled to close in the second half of 2026, to triple the market opportunity for its security and risk business. "ServiceNow's buying spree shows it's trying to get ahead of competitors on the orchestration ‚Äãand governance front by acquiring ‚ÄåIP, tech leadership, and customer bases," said Rebecca Wettemann, CEO of Valoir, an industry research and advisory firm. In recent months, ServiceNow ‚Äãhas bought security firm Veza, AI company Moveworks and sales automation platform Logik.ai, formerly ‚ÅÝLogik.io. The company closed its $2.85 billion acquisition ‚Äåof Moveworks this month. Armis, valued at $6.1 ‚ÄØbillion in a funding round in November, ‚Äãwas preparing for an initial public offering. The startup's latest funding was led by the alternative investment platform of Goldman Sachs. Existing ‚Äçinvestor CapitalG, Alphabet's VC arm, also participated in the round. Armis, founded in 2015, ‚ÅÝunveiled a three-year plan last month to reach $1 billion in annual recurring revenue after ‚Äãcrossing the $300 million milestone in ‚ÄåAugust.

data.title: Amazon's Zoox to recall 332 US vehicles over software error
data.description: WASHINGTON, ‚ÄåDec 23 : Amazon's self-driving unit Zoox said on Tuesday it is recalling 332 self-driving robotaxis because vehicles may cross the yellow center line and drive into or stop in front of oncoming traffic near intersections, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration said. Zoox said it has addressed the issue through a software update and said the issue did not result in any collisions. The ‚Äåcompany opened an investigation after¬Ýa Zoox robotaxi made a ‚Äåwide right turn in late August, crossed partially into the opposing travel lane, and temporarily stopped in front of the oncoming travel lane. Zoox identified 62 instances where a robotaxi crossed the lane line, partially or fully, unnecessarily. "We have proactively identified some instances where our vehicles were making maneuvers that, while common for human drivers, didn't meet ‚Äçour standards," Zoox said. Zoox said it engaged in ongoing conversations with NHTSA about the issue. In May, Zoox recalled 270 vehicles and issued a software update to improve how its vehicles track nearby pedestrians and prevent movement when someone is close after an unoccupied robotaxi ‚Äãwas involved in an April ‚Äå8 crash with a passenger car in Las Vegas. In April, the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration¬Ýclosed a probe into 258 Zoox vehicles¬Ýover a braking issue ‚Äãafter Zoox issued a recall to update their software to address an issue that could ‚ÅÝcause unexpected hard braking. NHTSA in August ‚Äåcertified Zoox vehicles for demonstration use and closed a probe, which the U.S. auto ‚Äãsafety regulator began in 2022, into whether they had complied with federal requirements. Other automakers have issued recalls over software issues in self-driving vehicles.¬Ý Alphabet ‚Äçunit Waymo earlier this month recalled its¬Ýself-driving vehicles after Texas officials said they illegally passed ‚ÅÝschool buses at least 19 times since the start of the school year. NHTSA opened a ‚Äãprobe¬Ýin October into Waymo vehicles¬Ýnear ‚Äåschool buses.

data.title: US dollar trims losses after stronger-than-expected growth data
data.description: NEW ‚ÄåYORK, Dec 23 : The U.S. dollar curbed its losses against the yen and euro on Tuesday, after data showed the world's largest economy posted higher-than-expected ‚Äågrowth in the third ‚Äåquarter, reinforcing expectations the Federal Reserve will pause cutting rates at its January meeting. Data showed that U.S. gross domestic product rose at ‚Äça 4.3 per cent annualized rate in the last quarter, the first estimate from the Commerce Department's Bureau of Economic ‚ÄãAnalysis showed. ‚ÄåEconomists polled by Reuters had forecast GDP would rise at ‚Äãa 3.3 per cent pace in the third ‚ÅÝquarter. Following the report, ‚Äåthe dollar cut its losses ‚Äãagainst the yen to 156.11 yen, still down 0.6 per cent. The ‚Äçeuro also slid, paring gains against ‚ÅÝthe dollar to $1.1786, still up 0.2 per cent ‚Äãon the day. (Reporting ‚Äåby Gertrude Chavez-Dreyfuss; ‚ÄçEditing ‚Äãby Chizu Nomiyama )

data.title: Orsted sells 55% of Taiwan wind farm to Cathay
data.description: COPENHAGEN, ‚ÄåDec 23 : Danish offshore wind developer Orsted has agreed ‚Äåto sell a ‚Äå55 per cent stake in its Greater Changhua 2 offshore ‚Äçwind farm in Taiwan to¬Ýlife insurance company ‚ÄãCathay ‚Äåin a deal worth ‚Äãaround 5 billion ‚ÅÝDanish crowns ($788.74 ‚Äåmillion), ‚Äãit said on Tuesday. ($1 = 6.3392 ‚ÄçDanish crowns) (Reporting ‚ÅÝby Stine ‚ÄãJacobsen, editing ‚Äåby ‚ÄçEssi ‚ÄãLehto)

data.title: AI data centers are forcing dirty ‚Äòpeaker‚Äô power plants back into service
data.description: Dec 23, CHICAGO - In Chicago‚Äôs working-class Pilsen neighborhood, a 60s-era oil-fired power ‚Äåplant rises up from an industrial lot behind Dvorak Park, which in warmer weather is packed with children climbing on its colorful playground and zooming down slides. The rarely-used eight-unit Fisk power plant owned by Houston-based NRG Energy was scheduled to retire next year. But then came electricity demands from artificial intelligence.¬Ý Prices shot up in the country‚Äôs biggest power market ‚Äì PJM Interconnection ‚Äì as electricity requests from data centers exceeded existing supplies, sounding the alarm over power shortfalls, and making Fisk and other plants like it suddenly profitable. "We believe there's an economic case to keep them around, so we withdrew the retirement notice,‚Äù said Matt Pistner, senior vice president of generation at NRG, of Fisk‚Äôs eight power-generating units.¬Ý¬Ý The Fisk power plant is among a growing number of so-called ‚Äúpeaker‚Äù electric generating units being pressed into duty across the U.S. as the nation‚Äôs electrical grids struggle to keep up with growing demand from data centers powering Big Tech‚Äôs investments in artificial intelligence. Peakers, which are meant to run only in short bursts during periods of spiking electricity demand, help stave off blackouts by supplying power on a moment‚Äôs notice. But there‚Äôs a trade-off: these often decades-old, fossil-fueled facilities emit more pollution when they are running and cost more to produce electricity than continuous power plants. A Reuters analysis of filings with the country‚Äôs biggest power grid shows that about 60 per cent of oil, gas and coal power plants slated for retirement in PJM postponed or cancelled those plans this year. Most of the plants averting shutdowns are peaker units.¬Ý The Fisk peakers were built on the site of a now-defunct coal-fired electricity generating station that operated for over a century. After years of fierce opposition by local residents, the coal plant shut more than a decade ago, but eight peaking units that run on petroleum oil continue to operate on the site. ‚ÄúWhen we found out that the coal plant was closing but there was still going to be power produced at the site, it was very disappointing,‚Äù said Jerry ‚ÄåMead-Lucero, a longtime advocate for the closure of the Fisk coal station who spent most of his adult life in Pilsen. Following the coal plant closure, pollution plummeted, but it didn‚Äôt vanish. Sulfur dioxide ranged from about 2 to as much as 25 tons per year from the site, ‚Äåaccording to the Environmental Protection Agency, as the eight-unit peaker plant occasionally lumbered to life to feed the grid. "That's not an insignificant amount considering the low chimneys and homes nearby," said Brian Urbaszewski, Director of Environmental Health Programs for Respiratory Health Association, an Illinois nonprofit that focuses on helping people with respiratory disease.¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý¬Ý DIRTY POWER Because they were built for speed instead of efficiency, peakers often do not have pollution controls like mercury scrubbers, which remove the toxic chemical from the power plants‚Äô emissions, and filters for particulate matter, according to academic and federal government research.¬Ý Some also have lower smokestacks, or chimneys, environmental advocates say,¬Ý meaning pollution can be more concentrated locally. Keeping peakers running longer may accelerate under U.S. President Donald Trump‚Äôs administration, which said it was exploring ways to tap into existing power sources, including peaker plants and other emergency systems, to quickly meet the massive new electricity demand. ‚ÄúThere are a ton of peaker plants that could operate more,‚Äù U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright told Reuters in an interview in September, adding that clean air regulations have kept more from running more frequently. ‚ÄúThe biggest targets are spare capacity on the grid today.‚Äù While peaker plants contribute about 3 per cent of the country‚Äôs power, they have the total capacity to produce 19 per cent, according to a report by the U.S. Government Accountability Office. Tapping into that spare capacity, however, could mean more harmful emissions being spewed into neighborhoods that are often already overburdened with environmental hazards. The country's roughly 1,000 peaker plants are disproportionately located in low-income communities of color, according to academic and federal government research, meaning that extending the plants‚Äô lives could leave vulnerable Americans to bear the brunt of more pollution. A 2022 study of formerly ‚Äúredlined‚Äù U.S. communities, which were cut off from financial services like mortgages for being predominantly Black or immigrant, found that residents were 53 per cent ‚Äçmore likely to have had a peaker plant built nearby since the year 2000 than in non-redlined areas. ‚ÄúIf you were a redlined neighborhood, you were more likely to have a fossil fuel power plant built nearby, and we saw that relationship was even stronger for peaker plants,‚Äù said UCLA professor of environmental health sciences Lara Cushing, who led the study.¬Ý POWER DEMANDS STRAINING THE GRID Most of the country's peaker plants were built during two periods of growth in energy consumption: in the mid-20th century as electrical appliances became common household items, and at the turn of the millennium as the economy grew and computers gained popularity. Afterwards, as energy-sapping devices and infrastructure became more efficient, U.S. power demand waned and many fossil-fired power plants shut. Meanwhile, solar and wind farms, which only produce power when the sun is shining and the wind is blowing, began to supply more of the country‚Äôs energy. "We're kind of making the old system work harder and that's part of why we're seeing this increased use of plants operating as peakers," said Frank Rusco, a director with the Government Accountability Office, which was directed by U.S. Congress, at the urging of environmental justice groups, to study the use of peaker plants and how they intersect with American communities.¬Ý The study found that natural gas peaker plants emit 1.6 times more sulfur dioxide for each unit of electricity produced on a median basis compared to non-peaker¬Ýplants. Fisk ‚Äãis part of the nation‚Äôs largest electrical grid, PJM Interconnection, which stretches across 13 states and covers the world‚Äôs biggest concentration of data centers. Demand from AI ‚Äådata centers is threatening to engulf the grid‚Äôs power reserves, and it is already driving up prices.¬Ý¬Ý Prices paid to power suppliers in PJM to ensure plants run at times of spiking demand soared by more than 800 per cent this summer, compared to a year earlier. That made owning peaker power plants much more lucrative. ‚ÄúIt is clear today, nationally, that electricity demand is outstripping supply ‚Äì the market reflects this, and generators are responding,‚Äù PJM spokesman Jeff Shields said. ‚ÄúWe cannot afford to lose existing generation while we continue to bring on new generation to keep pace with the electricity needs of data centers and other large loads powering the country‚Äôs economy.‚Äù About 23 oil, gas and coal power plants in PJM territory were scheduled to retire starting in 2025 or shortly after, ‚Äãaccording to a Reuters analysis of letters sent to PJM Interconnection by power companies. Since January, U.S. power companies, the grid operator, and the federal government have delayed or cancelled the retirements of 13 of those power plants, the letters showed. Of those plants that averted closure, 11 were peakers. Among those delayed were the roughly 55-year-old units at the ‚ÄúEddystone‚Äù plant outside of Philadelphia, owned by Constellation Energy, which were ordered to keep running by the Department of Energy. The Wagner ‚ÅÝpeaker near Baltimore, meanwhile, was kept alive at the request of PJM while the grid operator coordinates on the transmission needed for the removal of the generator. Many of the retained power ‚Äåplants were built as peakers, while others were initially intended to be around-the-clock power, but later downgraded to run only during emergencies. ‚ÄòLAST LINE OF DEFENSE‚Äô Fisk owner NRG Energy says peakers are essential safeguards for the grid that are being called on more often not just for data centers but for the electrification of manufacturing and transportation, and to avert blackouts caused by increasingly severe winter storms and summer heatwaves. Having the Fisk peakers in the ‚Äãcity means that Chicago doesn‚Äôt need to import electricity in the case of an emergency when outside power sources go down. ‚ÄúThey really are the last line of defense, and the shock absorber, for the system,‚Äù said Matt Pistner of NRG Energy. ‚ÄúWhen they‚Äôre needed, there is no other place to go.‚Äù While NRG owns power generating sources from nuclear energy to wind and solar, oil-fired peakers add another layer of certainty by ensuring the power fuel source can be stored on site, Pistner said. ‚ÄúDuring its run times, the power plant consistently operates within federal and state environmental regulations ‚Äî and we are proud of its record," an NRG spokesman told Reuters separately.¬Ý Energy experts say there are alternatives to peakers. Investing in more robust transmission lines could ‚Äçtransport electricity from parts of the country with oversupplies of power to those with shortfalls.¬Ý "If we do that, the system would run more efficiently and you would probably have a reduction in the amount of reliance on peakers," said the GAO's Rusco. Batteries, which are undergoing technological improvements to store power for longer, could also replace many peaker units, according to clean energy advocates. In the meantime, as AI power demand rises, communities like Pilsen, which have successfully ‚ÅÝfought to close some pollution sources in recent history, may find peaker plants more difficult to fight. ‚ÄúIt all adds up to significant cost increases for electricity consumers and significant increases in local pollution and will pre

Batteries, which are undergoing technological improvements to store power for longer, could also replace many peaker units, according to clean energy advocates. In the meantime, as AI power demand rises, communities like Pilsen, which have successfully ‚ÅÝfought to close some pollution sources in recent history, may find peaker plants more difficult to fight. ‚ÄúIt all adds up to significant cost increases for electricity consumers and significant increases in local pollution and will pre

data.title: Paramount's new offer for Warner Bros is not sufficient, major investor says
data.description: Dec 23 : Paramount Skydance's latest¬Ýoffer to buy Warner Bros Discovery still is not good enough for prominent shareholder Harris Oakmark, the investor told Reuters on Friday.¬Ý Warner Bros' fifth largest shareholder, owning 96 million shares or about¬Ý4 per cent of shares as of the end of September, said it would hold out for more from the Ellison family-controlled Paramount. "The changes in Paramount‚Äôs new offer were necessary, but not sufficient," Harris Oakmark portfolio manager and Director of U.S. Research Alex Fitch said in an email to Reuters. "We see the two deals as a toss-up, and there is a cost to changing paths. If Paramount is serious about ‚Äåwinning, they‚Äôre going to need to provide a greater incentive." Paramount on Monday amended its $108.4 billion ‚Äåhostile bid for the storied Hollywood studio to bolster its financing. Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison, whose son David owns Paramount, is now personally guaranteeing $40.4 billion of the bid to secure Warner Bros, which owns HBO Max and controls the Harry Potter, Lord of the Rings and Superman franchises. Questions about the financing, much of which was held in a revocable trust, had some Warner Bros investors unsure whether they would accept the offer. Paramount also increased the fee it will pay to $5.8 billion from $5 billion if regulators don't approve the deal, to match a competing offer ‚Äçfrom Netflix , although it didn't raise its $30-a-share bid.¬Ý 'TOP SHELF MEDIA ASSETS' Warner Bros investors now have until January 21, extended from January 8, to accept or reject the so-called tender offer. The board of Warner Bros unanimously recommended on Wednesday that shareholders reject Paramount's earlier bid in favor of Netflix's offer, saying the financing didn't provide a "full backstop."¬Ý Even though Netflix's cash offer of $23.25 a share is lower, the board said its bid was superior ‚Äãbecause the financing was more secure and it includes $4.50 ‚Äåin shares of Netflix common stock as well as whatever Warner Bros can get when it spins out Discovery Global as part of the deal.¬Ý The bidding war speaks to the quality of Warner Bros assets, said Yussef Gheriani, chief investment ‚Äãofficer of Chicago investment firm IHT Wealth Management, which owns 16,000 shares of Warner Bros, 6,500 shares of Netflix and 60,000 of Paramount. "It‚Äôs really rare ‚ÅÝto get an opportunity to add top shelf media assets to ‚Äåyour portfolio," he said, adding that he'll likely follow the board's advice on the sale. "They know the business inside out and have ‚Äãa better grasp of the nuances associated with the deal than we do." Investor Thomas Poehling, who owns 484,000 shares of Warner Bros and 639,000 of Paramount, said he'll likely take the revised offer if Netflix doesn't counter because Paramount ‚Äçhas a better chance of winning approval from regulators.¬Ý Ellison's guarantee "adds a lot of stability to that offer and that removes a lot of the ‚ÅÝfinancing uncertainty," he said. Gheriani and Poehling aren't the only investors to hold shares in the rival movie studios. Vanguard, State Street and BlackRock are Warner Bros' three largest ‚Äãshareholders, controlling at least 22 per cent of the ‚Äåcompany between them. All three are also among the top ten investors in Paramount and Netflix. None commented for ‚Äçthis ‚Äãarticle.

data.title: Thailand eyes measures on 'huge' gold trading amid baht surge
data.description: BANGKOK, Dec 23 : Thailand's finance ministry is considering a tax on online gold transactions and will explore measures to limit trading volumes by the biggest traders, officials said on Tuesday, as they try to tackle a surge in the baht currency.¬Ý The baht has gained by 10.3 per cent against the dollar so far this year to become Asia's best-performing currency, and it is now at its highest level against the dollar in more than four years. The baht's rapid appreciation has undermined the competitiveness of the export and tourism sectors in Southeast Asia's second-largest economy, which has been struggling with¬Ýmultiple challenges like¬ÝU.S. tariffs, high¬Ýhousehold debt, a border conflict¬Ýwith Cambodia and¬Ýpolitical uncertainty¬Ýahead of¬Ýelections on February 8. Traders selling ‚Äågold, which has climbed more than 70 per cent this year, in dollars in foreign markets are then selling ‚Äåthe U.S. currency for baht. Senior finance ministry official Lavaron Sangsnit said the revenue department would look into imposing a specific business tax on gold trading conducted via online platforms, and would also consider new rules to force such platforms to report transaction data to tax authorities, similar to existing e-commerce platforms. The central bank will also study measures to cap the volume of transactions on digital gold trading platforms, he said. GOLD TAX COULD HURT SECTOR, MIGHT NOT COOL BAHT SURGE Gold traders have expressed their opposition to any tax on gold trading, saying it would harm the sector. "If a tax is imposed, gold trading will likely ‚Äçdecline, and there could be a broad impact as online trading is very large," said Jitti Tangsithpakdi,¬Ýpresident of the Thai Gold Traders Association.¬Ý "There was no mention of this yesterday," he said, referring to a meeting the association held with the central bank. Poon Panichpibool, market strategist at Krungthai Bank, said that while the measures might help the baht somewhat, they would not solve the problem. "Even if a tax is imposed, if people still see upside, they will keep trading," he said.¬Ý "It depends ‚Äãon how strong the upside for gold will be. ‚ÄåIf I have to predict, next year gold trading will likely drop anyway because the upside is reducing". BAHT'S RISE 'NOT REFLECTING ECONOMIC FUNDAMENTALS' The Thai baht's gains against the U.S. dollar are being driven by "huge" gold trading volumes, with its rise not in line with fundamentals, ‚ÄãCentral Bank Governor Vitai Ratanakorn told the same briefing. "The trading volumes are immense, and the baht's rise is not reflecting economic fundamentals," he said, adding gold transactions by ‚ÅÝlarge traders could be equivalent to¬Ý 50 per cent of GDP this year.¬Ý "We ‚Äåhave to admit that the strong baht is impacting the economy. Our current approach is very proactive," Vitai said. The central bank will manage unusually large ‚Äãgold transactions conducted in baht and expects to set limits on gold trading volumes for major traders around the middle of next month, Vitai said, adding the measures will not affect traditional gold shop transactions. Vitai reiterated that there were no plans to impose ‚Äçany aggressive capital control measures and said the central bank was hopeful that the measures on gold trading would help ease the currency's strength. The central bank ‚ÅÝsaid earlier that it had moved to contain the rapid gains in the baht, ordering tighter scrutiny of foreign exchange transactions linked to gold trading and instructing commercial banks to ‚Äãclosely monitor foreign currency inflows. Earlier, Finance Minister Ekniti ‚ÄåNitithanprapas said the baht was too strong and was hurting Thailand's economy.

data.title: Japan to test mine rare-earth mud from deep seabed¬Ý
data.description: TOKYO: ‚ÄåJapan will conduct test mining of rare-earth-rich mud from the deep seabed off Minamitori Island, some 1,900km southeast of Tokyo, from Jan 11 to Feb 14, the head of the government-backed project said on Tuesday (Dec 23).¬Ý The operation will mark the world's first attempt to continuously lift rare-earth mud from a depth of around 6,000m ‚Äåon to a vessel. Tokyo, like its Western allies, is ‚Äåseeking to secure stable supplies of critical minerals as China, the dominant supplier of rare earths, tightens export controls.¬Ý "One of our missions is to build a supply chain for domestically produced rare earths to ensure stable supply of minerals essential to industry," Shoichi Ishii, program director of the Cabinet Office's national ‚Äçplatform for innovative ocean developments, told reporters. The Japanese government is pressing ahead with a national project as part of broader efforts to strengthen maritime and economic security. The January test will focus on connecting the deep-sea mining system and confirming its ‚Äãability to lift 350 metric ‚Äåtonnes of rare-earth mud per day. Environmental impacts will be monitored both onboard and on the seabed throughout the operation. No production target ‚Äãhas been set, but if successful, a full-scale mining trial will be conducted in ‚ÅÝFebruary 2027. The government-funded project has spent ‚Äåabout ¬•40 billion (US$256 million) since 2018, Ishii said, though estimated reserves ‚Äãhave not been disclosed. Ishii also said that while their research vessel was conducting rare earth surveys within Japan's exclusive economic zone (EEZ) around ‚ÄçMinamitori Island from May 27 to Jun 25, a Chinese naval fleet entered ‚ÅÝthe waters on Jun 7. "We feel a strong sense of crisis that such intimidating actions ‚Äãwere taken, despite our ‚Äåactivities being limited to seabed resource surveys within our EEZ," ‚Äçhe ‚Äãsaid.

data.title: Amazon's Zoox to recall 332 US vehicles over software error, NHTSA says
data.description: Dec ‚Äå23 : Amazon's self-driving unit Zoox is recalling 332 vehicles in the ‚ÄåU.S. over ‚Äåan Automated Driving Systems software error that may cause ‚Äçthe vehicles to cross or stop in front ‚Äãof oncoming ‚Äåtraffic, increasing the risk ‚Äãof a crash, ‚ÅÝthe U.S. ‚ÄåNational Highway ‚ÄãTraffic Safety Administration said on ‚ÄçTuesday.

data.title: Toyota to recall over 55,000 US vehicles over loose inverter connection, NHTSA says
data.description: Dec ‚Äå23 : Toyota is recalling 55,405 vehicles in the U.S. as ‚Äåbolt inside ‚Äåthe inverter may not have been tightened properly, ‚Äçcausing incomplete contact at the inverter terminal, ‚Äãthe U.S. ‚ÄåNational Highway Traffic Safety ‚ÄãAdministration said on ‚ÅÝTuesday. The ‚Äåremedy is ‚Äãcurrently under development, NHTSA ‚Äçsaid.

data.title: Samsung Electronics unit Harman to acquire ZF Group's ADAS business for $1.8 billion
data.description: SEOUL, Dec ‚Äå23 : Samsung Electronics said on Tuesday its unit Harman International has agreed to acquire German automotive supplier ZF Friedrichshafen's advanced driver assistance system (ADAS) business for 1.5 billion euros ($1.77 billion). Samsung said ‚Äåthe deal will allow Harman ‚Äåto secure ADAS-related technologies and products, including front-facing vehicle cameras and ADAS controllers, marking a full-scale entry into the fast-growing ADAS market. ADAS technologies use cameras and sensors to ‚Äçdetect the surrounding environment, including lanes, the distance to vehicles ahead and pedestrians to assist safe driving. The company said the market ‚Äãfor ADAS and ‚Äåcentral vehicle controllers is expected to expand in size from 62.6 trillion ‚Äãwon ($42.18 billion) in 2025 to 97.4 trillion won ‚ÅÝin 2030. The acquisition marks ‚ÄåSamsung's latest major deal this year, ‚Äãafter it announced in May a 1.5 billion euro agreement to buy ‚ÄçGerman cooling system maker FlaktGroup. Samsung said the ‚ÅÝtransaction is expected to close within 2026. ($1 = 1,484.1500 ‚Äãwon) ($1 = 0.8495 euros) (Reporting ‚Äåby Heekyong YangEditing by Ed ‚ÄçDavies ‚Äãand Shri Navaratnam)
